hints:
A Greek word meaning "youth," the term "Kouros" is generally applied to monumental sculptures from the Archaic period in ancient Greece that represented idealized male nude figures.  

They generally do *not* represent a God commonly depicted in Greek funerary art, and "Kouros" is also not the name of a specific individual.

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/daedalic-archaic/v/anavysos-kouros)**

choices.content: a type of monumental nude sculpture from the Archaic period in ancient Greece.
a god commonly depicted in Greek funerary art.

the name of the man portrayed in the sculpture below.  ![Anavysos Kouros, c. 530 B.C.E., marble, 6 feet 4 inches high (National Archaeological Museum, Athens)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ab2a37289abb5e206337c7ba229fd7d6.jpg)

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the function of Archaic Kouros figures?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Kouros figures were not portraits of specific individuals, but served as an idealized representation of the youthful male hero that Greek culture valued.  

In addition to functioning as grave markers, they sometimes served as religious offerings, and as a representation of the god Apollo.  

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/daedalic-archaic/v/anavysos-kouros)**

choices.content: They were offerings in religious sanctuaries.
They were representations of gods, usually Apollo.
They were portraits of soldiers who died in battle.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which detail is NOT characteristic to all archaic Kouros figures?**

![Anavysos Kouros, c. 530 B.C.E., marble, 6 feet 4 inches high (National Archaeological Museum, Athens)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/55af1c3b7b1829a9ceb048aabd962e84.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Although later figures display more naturalistic anatomy, all Archaic Kouroi share the same standing posture, even distribution of weight, serene smile, and distinctive hairstyle.  

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/daedalic-archaic/v/anavysos-kouros)**

choices.content: the figure's smile and facial expression
the braided headband and long hair
the naturalism and rounded anatomical forms

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the historical sequence of these sculptures?  Place the oldest sculpture at the top of the column and the most recent at the bottom.**

[[☃ orderer 1]]
hints:
Greek kouros figures show the influence of earlier Egyptian art (where the figures were not nude). So the oldest figure here is Egyptian and wears a kilt.

The next figure in chronological sequence is the New York Kouros, who we see entirely from the front and whose body is very block-like and geometric, and whose head is too large for his body.

The next figure in the sequence has more rounded forms and a smaller head. 

And the final figure (the most recent chronologically) stands in contrapposto, with his weight shifted to one leg.

Greek sculpture can be dated through a historical shift toward greater naturalism in anatomical features and postures.  Early figures appear stiff and frontal, while later figures display more integrated musculature and use of contrapposto.  

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/daedalic-archaic/v/anavysos-kouros)**
